pair.title: A True Life Hack: What Physical 'Life Force' Turns Biology's Wheels?

story.title: What Physical ‘Life Force’ Turns Biology’s Wheels? | Quanta Magazine

comments.text: This is a great deep dive into the flagellar motor, though if you're interested in the computational side of bacterial navigation, there's some fascinating work happening at https://github.com/chemotaxis-group/signal-processing that models the entire chemotaxis pathway. They've got some really clean simulations of how these motors integrate multiple sensory inputs in real time.

kids.text:
Thanks for the link! That's exactly the kind of work I wish I'd had space to dive into more. The signal processing angle is what makes this genuinely interesting to me-the motor itself is beautiful engineering, but the way bacteria integrate chemotactic signals in real time, with that stochastic switching between runs and tumbles, that's where the intelligence lives. The motor is just the executor.

I'll be honest though, I'm not deeply familiar with that specific repo. Is it actively maintained? I've been meaning to dig into some of the newer structural work from the cryo-EM folks (the 2023 papers on the stator-rotor interface were wild), but the computational models lag behind the structural biology by years usually. Would be great to see someone bridge that gap more systematically.

This reminds me of the broader question of how much of bacterial behavior is hardcoded versus learned/adaptive. The chemotaxis stuff is elegant, but I've always wondered whether we're anthropomorphizing the "signal integration" part. Like, is the bacterium actually computing something, or is it just a bunch of coupled protein interactions that happen to produce behavior that looks computational?

There's some older work on this by Bray and others that models the whole flagellar switch as just a chemical reaction network with no actual information processing, and the "tumble/run" switching emerges from the physics alone. Makes you question whether calling it "intelligence" is doing real work or just poetry. Still beautiful either way, but the distinction matters if you're thinking about origins of cognition.

The signal processing angle is indeed where it gets wild. What's less discussed is the actual rotational mechanics-the stator proteins (FliG, FliM, FliN) don't just respond to the proton gradient, they're doing something closer to a ratcheting mechanism that's still not fully mapped at atomic resolution.

The simulations are great pedagogically, but they tend to gloss over the cooperativity problem: how do ~26 stator units coordinate their conformational changes fast enough to maintain 300+ rpm without tearing the rotor apart? The latest cryo-EM structures from the Kükathasan lab are starting to show this, but the kinetics remain fuzzy. Real bacteria seem to solve it with maybe 10-15 active units at any moment, switching in and out stochastically.

If you want to see where the real gaps are, look at the temperature-dependence studies. The motor speeds up nonlinearly as you increase temp, suggesting there's a phase transition or critical threshold in the binding dynamics that we're still reverse-engineering. That's where the "optimally designed" claim gets interesting-it's not that evolution found a perfect solution, it's that this particular solution is robust to the noise inherent in a nanoscale machine operating in a thermal soup.

The chemotaxis group work is solid, but worth noting that Berg and Purcell's 1977 paper on bacterial chemotaxis still holds up remarkably well as a theoretical framework. What's changed is we can now actually validate their predictions at the molecular level with single-molecule tracking. The flagellar motor itself is almost a solved problem compared to how cells integrate temporal gradients across flagella.

comments.text: This reminds me of the mid-90s when I first read about the flagellar motor in a molecular biology textbook. The illustrations showed these impossibly intricate protein machines, and I remember thinking "how does anyone figure this out?" The answer turned out to be decades of X-ray crystallography, cryo-EM, and just... obsessive measurement of how fast these things spin. There's something almost quaint about how Wolchover frames it as "finally understood", the motor was discovered in the 70s, sure, but we've been gradually adding pieces to the puzzle for fifty years. The recent cryo-EM work (ResearchGate has some of the papers) finally gave us atomic-resolution views of the stator and rotor. What gets me is how *constrained* the design is. Billions of years of optimization squeezed out every inefficiency. Makes you wonder what we're missing in our own engineered systems by not having that kind of evolutionary pressure.

kids.text: The flagellar motor wasn't "finally understood" in the sense of being solved-we've had the basic rotary mechanism down since the 80s (Berg's work). What's new here is finer mechanistic detail, cryo-EM resolution of the stator-rotor interface, and quantitative models of energy transduction. The "50 years" framing conflates discovery with complete understanding, which is misleading. It's more like "we went from knowing it exists and spins to understanding the proton gradient coupling in exquisite detail." Different thing.

> It's more like "we went from knowing it exists and spins to understanding the proton gradient coupling in exquisite detail"

This is the real story, but Wolchover's framing sells better. I remember reading Berg's papers in the 80s when the rotary mechanism was already locked down, and the cryo-EM stuff from the last decade is genuinely impressive. But "50 years to finally understand" plays better in Quanta than "incremental refinement via better microscopes." The headline wouldn't get you to click if it said "we got clearer pictures of something we mostly knew."

The cryo-EM breakthrough was huge, but what's really wild is how much we still don't understand about the assembly process itself. The motor has about 20 different protein subunits, and they self-assemble in a specific order, but the molecular choreography, how does FliK know when to stop adding rings? How does the C-ring know its geometry without a template?, those questions are still pretty open.

The rotation mechanism itself is elegant once you see it: proton gradient drives conformational changes in FliM, which ratchets the rotor around the stator. But bacteria achieve this with maybe 10-12 stator units working asynchronously, whereas human rotary motors need hundreds of coils in perfect synchrony. Evolution just... solved it differently, and more economically.

If you want to see how the field is still moving, check out the work coming out of Baker's lab at UW on in situ structures. They're imaging these motors inside intact cells now, which is revealing that the isolated structures we get from cryo-EM might not tell the whole story about how the motor actually works under real osmotic stress.

> the motor was discovered in the 70s, sure, but "finally understood" is doing a lot of work here

This is the problem with popular science writing. Wolchover acts like we just cracked the code, when really we've been incrementally refining our understanding for 50 years. The flagellar motor isn't some mystery solved last month. We've had structural models since the 90s, detailed mechanistic work in the 2000s, and now we have better cryo-EM reconstructions. It's continuous progress, not a eureka moment.

And the framing about it being "literally perfect" and "optimal" is exactly the kind of thing that makes creationists go nuts. It's well-optimized through selection pressure, sure, but there's nothing magical about it. It's a proton pump coupled to a rotor. Elegant? Yes. Impossible to explain without invoking design? No. We can explain exactly how it evolved through gene duplication and exaptation.

The real story is boring compared to what Quanta wants to sell: that biology contains deep mysteries only now being unveiled. The flagellar motor is interesting because it's a real molecular machine that works through chemistry we can test and measure. Not because it's some ineffable life force finally revealed.

comments.text:
This feels like the resurgence of mechanistic biology that peaked in the 1950s-60s before we got distracted by molecular reductionism. Back then, people actually studied whole systems doing work, not just sequences. The flagellar motor is a perfect example of why that mattered.

What's interesting now is that if we took this level of scrutiny to, say, FDA approval of biotech, we'd probably reject half the candidates on the grounds that we don't understand their actual operating constraints. We approve based on statistical p-values in narrow assays, not on whether the molecule can actually maintain homeostasis under real physiological noise like bacteria have to handle every second of their lives.

The motor is optimal because it *has to be*. Humans get to be sloppy because we externalize our inefficiencies as pollution and electricity bills.

kids.text:
I think you're framing this as "mechanistic vs reductionist" but the real insight is that you need both simultaneously. The flagellar motor *is* a sequence, ion channels, rotor proteins, stator complexes, but understanding the sequence tells you almost nothing about why it rotates at 300 Hz or how it switches direction in 0.1 milliseconds. You have to study the constraints (viscosity, energetics, thermal noise) to understand why evolution converged on *this particular* molecular design and not a thousand others.

The FDA approval point cuts deeper though. We've gotten very good at measuring whether a drug binds protein X, but terrible at asking "does this system actually work under the metabolic pressures of a real human?" The flagellar motor succeeded because bacteria had to *move or starve* for a billion years. Most biotech candidates get tested for single-target efficacy in vitro, then we're shocked when the operating envelope in vivo is nothing like the bench results. You'd need to actually operate the system under constraint, not just sequence it.

I remember reading Denis Noble's work on heart rhythm in the 70s and thinking the same thing. He'd measure actual tissue under load, not just pull sequences. The flagellar motor reminds me of that approach - you can't predict 300 Hz rotation from protein names alone.

comments.text: This reads like someone discovered the flagellar motor exists and decided it proves design or something equally silly. The motor is elegant, sure, but "literally perfect"? It stalls, it gets clogged with debris, bacteria die constantly. Evolution found a good-enough solution that happened to work in water at microscopic scales. That's not perfection, that's local optimization in a specific niche.

kids.text: Actually the article's point is subtly different. Samuel's "optimal" refers to the thermodynamic efficiency of the motor itself, not the organism's fitness. The flagellar motor converts proton gradients to rotation at near-theoretical limits for a molecular machine, which is measurable and real. You're right that the bacterium as a whole isn't perfect (it dies, it gets stuck), but the motor protein complex is doing its job with minimal waste. That's what makes it interesting to study.

comments.text:
The flagellar motor's optimality is overstated a bit here. Berg and Purcell's 1977 paper showed it's efficient relative to diffusion, but "literally perfect" glosses over some real constraints. The motor wastes heat, the rotor has mechanical slip under load, and the stator proteins have limited duty cycles before they need replacement.

That said, the recent cryo-EM structures from Beeby's group and the single-molecule work by people like Demeyer have been genuinely revelatory. The way proton flow couples to mechanical rotation through the FliG/FliM interaction is elegant, and the finding that the C-ring can operate partially decoupled from the MS ring explains some of the robustness we see in vivo. Worth reading if you want to see how "good enough" biology actually implements near-optimal solutions under real metabolic budgets.

kids.text:
The "optimal" framing here is mostly PR for evolution. Yes, Berg and Purcell showed it beats diffusion, but that's a low bar when you're a microbe. Real optimality would mean zero slip, zero heat waste, infinite duty cycles. What we actually have is a motor that's good-enough, which is very different.

The deeper issue: these biological machines become canonical in textbooks precisely because they're *visible* under cryo-EM now. We don't talk about the thousands of cellular mechanisms that are mediocre or redundant because we can't see them clearly yet. Policy-wise, this shapes how we fund biology-we chase the photogenic, not the representative. Worth flagging when science journalism treats "we finally imaged it well" as proof of "nature's perfection."

comments.text: The creationist comparison is a bit of a tell. The flagellar motor is impressive, sure, but "literally perfect" glosses over the fact that many bacteria use simpler motors or don't have flagella at all. Evolution found a good solution, not an optimal one. The motor is also repurposed from ATP synthase, which suggests contingency more than design.

kids.text:
The ATP synthase connection is interesting but worth clarifying. The flagellar motor's rotor proteins (FliG, FliM, FliN) and stator proteins (MotA, MotB) are structurally distinct from ATP synthase components, even if they share some mechanistic principles around proton gradients. The homology is looser than often suggested.

That said, your broader point holds. Many proteobacteria use simpler motors (like the type III secretion system), and plenty of bacteria lack flagella entirely and rely on pili or passive drift. The "literally perfect" framing does flatten the evolutionary narrative. It's a well-optimized solution within constraints, not a global optimum.

I'd push back on the "contingency over design" framing. Yes, ATP synthase shares structural homology with the flagellar motor, but that's exactly how evolution works at scale, repurposing proven molecular machinery. The real policy question is why we're still discovering these mechanisms so late. We should be funding structural biology and cryo-EM at 10x current levels if we want to understand the design principles that actually work in nature. The creationists aren't wrong that it's elegant; they're just wrong about the implications.

The homology argument actually supports contingency, not design principles. ATP synthase evolved first (probably as a proton pump), and the flagellar motor borrowed its rotor-stator architecture. That's repurposing under constraint, not optimization. A "designed" system wouldn't reuse a proton gradient mechanism that was meant for something else. The elegance is real; the optimality claim isn't.
